en.extract:
The Rio Madeira stipplethroat (Epinecrophylla amazonica) is a species of bird in subfamily Thamnophilinae of family Thamnophilidae, the "typical antbirds". It is found in Bolivia, Brazil, and Peru. In the past it has also been called eastern stipple-throated antwren, Madeira stipple-throated antwren, Rio Madeira antwren, and Madeira antwren.


Taxonomy and systematics
The Rio Madeira stipplethroat was long treated as a subspecies of the rufous-backed stipplethroat (previously called rufous-backed antwren Myrmotherula haematonota, now Epinecrophylla haematonota). Beginning in 2014 taxonomists recognized it as a full species.
The Rio Madeira stipplethroat has two subspecies, the nominate E. a. amazonica (von Ihering, 1905) and E. a. dentei (Whitney, BM et al., 2013). Subspecies E. a. dentei was originally described as a species but was soon recognized in its current placement.


Description
The Rio Madeira stipplethroat is 10 to 11 cm (3.9 to 4.3 in) long and weighs 8.7 to 9 g (0.31 to 0.32 oz). Adult males of the nominate subspecies have a mottled yellowish brown face and a white throat with black streaks. They have a dark yellowish brown crown, a slightly redder nape, and a dark red mantle, back, rump, and uppertail coverts. Their tail is olive-gray. Their primary wing coverts are blackish with reddish tips, the rest of the wing coverts dark brown with yellowish buff tips, and the flight feathers dark red. Their underparts are gray. Their iris is brownish red. Adult females have duller red upperparts than males. Their throat is dull yellow-brown with obscure dark marks. Their underparts are pale yellowish brown. Their iris is grayish or pale beige. Both sexes have a dark gray bill, legs, and feet. Males of subspecies E. a. dentei have a buffy throat with black streaks and a tail that is almost as red as the back. Females have a paler and grayer belly than the nominate.


Distribution and habitat
The nominate subspecies E. a. amazonica of the Rio Madeira stipplethroat is found in south-central Amazonian Brazil from the watershed of the Juruá River east to the Madeira River, in Peru's Department of Madre de Dios, and in far northern Bolivia's Pando Department. Subspecies E. a. dentei is found in Brazil in the watershed of the Aripuanã and Machado rivers east of the Madeira. The area spans southern Amazonas, northwestern Mato Grosso, and northeastern Rondônia states. The species primarily inhabits lowland terra firme evergreen forest. In Bolivia it also occurs in more upland forest. In elevation it ranges only up to 400 m (1,300 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The Rio Madeira stipplethroat is believed to be a year-round resident throughout its range.


Feeding
The Rio Madeira stipplethroat feeds on arthropods, especially insects and spiders. It typically forages singly, in pairs, or in small family groups, and usually as part of a mixed-species feeding flock. It mostly forages in the forest understory up to about 3 m (10 ft) above the ground. It takes its prey almost entirely by gleaning from dead leaves on trees but also from dead leaves caught in vine tangles and small palms.


Breeding
Nothing is known about the Rio Madeira stipplethroat's breeding biology.


Vocalization
The Rio Madeira stipplethroat's song is similar to that of the rufous-backed stipplethroat, which is  an "extr. high, very thin, descending, rapid shiver 'tsititi---' " that lasts two or three seconds but slower and with longer notes and intervals between them. Its calls appear to be the same as the rufous-backed's, a "doublet of abrupt notes, second higher-pitched", an "abrupt single note", and a "high-pitched rattle".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the Rio Madeira stipplethroat as being of Least Concern. It has a large range; its population size is not known and is believed to be stable. No immediate threats have been identified. It appears to be fairly common throughout its range, "which contains large, contiguous expanses of intact habitat which, although not formally protected, appear to be at minimal risk of development in the near term".


References

The '''Rio Madeira stipplethroat''' ('''''Epinecrophylla amazonica''''') is a species of [[bird]] in subfamily Thamnophilinae of family [[Thamnophilidae]], the "typical antbirds".<ref name=IOC14.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/antbirds/ |title=Antbirds |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 14.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=January 2024 |access-date=January 4, 2024 }}</ref> It is found in [[Bolivia]], [[Brazil]], and [[Peru]].<ref name=SACCcountries>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 26 November 2023. Version 3. IOC World Bird List (v 10.1). Doi 10.14344/IOC.ML.10.1.  http://www.worldbirdnames.org/</ref><!-- -->, and '''Madeira antwren'''<ref name=Clements2019>Clements, J. F., et al. 2018. The eBird/Clements checklist of birds of the world: v2018. Downloaded from https://www.birds.cornell.edu/clementschecklist/download/ retrieved August 20, 2018</ref><!-- -->.

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The Rio Madeira stipplethroat was long treated as a subspecies of the [[rufous-backed stipplethroat]] (previously called rufous-backed antwren ''Myrmotherula haematonota'', now ''Epinecrophylla haematonota''). Beginning in 2014 taxonomists recognized it as a full species.<ref name=Whitney2013>{{ cite book | last1=Whitney | first1=B.M. | last2=Isler | first2=M.L. | last3=Bravo | first3=G.A. | last4=Aristizábal | first4=N. | last5=Schunck | first5=F. | last6=Silveira | first6=L.F. | last7=Piacentini | first7=V.Q. | year=2013 | chapter=A new species of ''Epinecrophylla'' antwren from the Aripuanã–Machado interfluvium in central Amazonian Brazil with revision of the "stipple-throated antwren" complex | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | editor4-last=Christie | editor4-first=D.A. | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World. Special Volume: New Species and Global Index | publisher=Lynx Edicions | place=Barcelona | pages=263–267 | chapter-url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/262697925 }}</ref><ref name=Isler>{{ cite web | last=Isler | first=Morton | title=Proposal (589A): Split ''Epinecrophylla haematonota'' into four species | url=http://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCprop589.htm | publisher=South American Classification Committee of the American Ornithological Society | date=November 2013 | access-date=23 February 2018 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/updates/archives-4-1-to-4-4/species-4-1-to-4-4/|title=IOC Species Updates v.4.2|last=|first=|date=|website=|publisher=|access-date=October 3, 2016}}</ref><ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 26 November 2023. A classification of the bird species of South America. American Ornithological Society. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved November 27, 2023</ref>

The Rio Madeira stipplethroat has two subspecies, the [[Nominate subspecies|nominate]] ''E. a. amazonica'' ([[Hermann von Ihering|von Ihering]], 1905) and ''E. a. dentei'' ([https://species.wikimedia.org/wiki/Bret_M._Whitney Whitney, BM] et al., 2013).<ref name=IOC14.1/> Subspecies ''E. a. dentei'' was originally [[Species description|described]] as a species<ref name=Whitney2013/> but was soon recognized in its current placement.<ref name=Isler/><ref name=SACClist/>

==Description==

The Rio Madeira stipplethroat is {{convert|10|to|11|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|8.7|to|9|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. Adult males of the nominate subspecies have a mottled yellowish brown face and a white throat with black streaks. They have a dark yellowish brown crown, a slightly redder nape, and a dark red mantle, back, rump, and uppertail [[coverts]]. Their tail is olive-gray. Their primary wing [[coverts]] are blackish with reddish tips, the rest of the wing coverts dark brown with yellowish buff tips, and the flight feathers dark red. Their underparts are gray. Their iris is brownish red. Adult females have duller red upperparts than males. Their throat is dull yellow-brown with obscure dark marks. Their underparts are pale yellowish brown. Their iris is grayish or pale beige. Both sexes have a dark gray bill, legs, and feet. Males of subspecies ''E. a. dentei'' have a buffy throat with black streaks and a tail that is almost as red as the back. Females have a paler and grayer belly than the nominate.<ref name=RMST-BOW>del Hoyo, J., N. Collar, and G. M. Kirwan (2020). Rio Madeira Stipplethroat (''Epinecrophylla amazonica''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.madant1.01 retrieved January 13, 2024</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The nominate subspecies ''E. a. amazonica'' of the Rio Madeira stipplethroat is found in south-central [[Amazon Basin|Amazonian]] Brazil from the watershed of the [[Juruá River]] east to the [[Madeira River]], in Peru's [[Department of Madre de Dios]], and in far northern Bolivia's [[Pando Department]].<ref name=RMST-BOW/><ref name=Clements2023>Clements, J. F., P.C. Rasmussen, T. S. Schulenberg, M. J. Iliff, T. A. Fredericks, J. A. Gerbracht, D. Lepage, A. Spencer, S. M. Billerman, B. L. Sullivan, and C. L. Wood. 2023. The eBird/Clements checklist of birds of the world: v2023. Downloaded from https://www.birds.cornell.edu/clementschecklist/download/ retrieved October 28, 2023</ref> Subspecies ''E. a. dentei'' is found in Brazil in the watershed of the [[Aripuanã River|Aripuanã]] and Machado rivers east of the Madeira. The area spans southern [[Amazonas (Brazilian state)|Amazonas]], northwestern [[Mato Grosso]], and northeastern [[Rondônia]] states. The species primarily inhabits lowland ''[[Forest#Tropical moist|terra firme]]'' [[evergreen forest]]. In Bolivia it also occurs in more upland forest. In elevation it ranges only up to {{convert|400|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=RMST-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The Rio Madeira stipplethroat is believed to be a year-round resident throughout its range.<ref name=RMST-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The Rio Madeira stipplethroat feeds on [[arthropod]]s, especially insects and spiders. It typically forages singly, in pairs, or in small family groups, and usually as part of a [[mixed-species feeding flock]]. It mostly forages in the forest understory up to about {{convert|3|m|ft|sigfig=1|abbr=on}} above the ground. It takes its prey almost entirely by gleaning from dead leaves on trees but also from dead leaves caught in vine tangles and small palms.<ref name=RMST-BOW/>

===Breeding===

Nothing is known about the Rio Madeira stipplethroat's breeding biology.<ref name=RMST-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Epinecrophylla-amazonica |species=Rio Madeira stipplethroat}}

===Vocalization===

The Rio Madeira stipplethroat's song is similar to that of the rufous-backed stipplethroat, which is  an "extr. high, very thin, descending, rapid shiver 'tsititi---' " that lasts two or three seconds<ref name=vanPerlo>{{cite book | last =van Perlo | first = Ber| title =A Field Guide to the Birds of Brazil | publisher =Oxford University Press | date =2009 | location =New York | pages =202–203 | isbn =978-0-19-530155-7 }}</ref> but slower and with longer notes and intervals between them.<ref name=RMST-BOW/> Its calls appear to be the same as the rufous-backed's, a "doublet of abrupt notes, second higher-pitched", an "abrupt single note", and a "high-pitched rattle".<ref name=RMST-BOW/><ref name=RBST-BOW>Zimmer, K., M.L. Isler, and G. M. Kirwan (2020). Rufous-backed Stipplethroat (''Epinecrophylla haematonota''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.rubsti1.01 retrieved January 12, 2024</ref>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the Rio Madeira stipplethroat as being of Least Concern. It has a large range; its population size is not known and is believed to be stable. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It appears to be fairly common throughout its range, "which contains large, contiguous expanses of intact habitat which, although not formally protected, appear to be at minimal risk of development in the near term".<ref name=RMST-BOW/>
